speaker: THERSITES
text: How now, Thersites? What, lost in the

speaker: THERSITES
text: labyrinth of thy fury? Shall the elephant Ajax carry

speaker: THERSITES
text: it thus? He beats me, and I rail at him. O, worthy

speaker: THERSITES
text: satisfaction! Would it were otherwise, that I could

speaker: THERSITES
text: beat him whilst he railed at me. ’Sfoot, I’ll learn to

speaker: THERSITES
text: conjure and raise devils but I’ll see some issue of

speaker: THERSITES
text: my spiteful execrations. Then there’s Achilles, a

speaker: THERSITES
text: rare enginer! If Troy be not taken till these two undermine

speaker: THERSITES
text: it, the walls will stand till they fall of

speaker: THERSITES
text: themselves. O thou great thunder-darter of Olympus,

speaker: THERSITES
text: forget that thou art Jove, the king of gods;

speaker: THERSITES
text: and, Mercury, lose all the serpentine craft of thy

speaker: THERSITES
text: caduceus, if you take not that little, little, less than

speaker: THERSITES
text: little wit from them that they have, which short-armed

speaker: THERSITES
text: ignorance itself knows is so abundant

speaker: THERSITES
text: scarce it will not in circumvention deliver a fly

speaker: THERSITES
text: from a spider without drawing their massy irons

speaker: THERSITES
text: and cutting the web. After this, the vengeance on

speaker: THERSITES
text: the whole camp! Or rather, the Neapolitan bone-ache!

speaker: THERSITES
text: For that, methinks, is the curse depending

speaker: THERSITES
text: on those that war for a placket. I have said my

speaker: THERSITES
text: prayers, and devil Envy say “Amen.”—What ho,

speaker: THERSITES
text: my lord Achilles!

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Who’s there? Thersites? Good

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Thersites, come in and rail.

speaker: THERSITES
text: If I could ’a remembered a gilt counterfeit,

speaker: THERSITES
text: thou couldst not have slipped out of my contemplation.

speaker: THERSITES
text: But it is no matter. Thyself upon thyself! The

speaker: THERSITES
text: common curse of mankind, folly and ignorance,

speaker: THERSITES
text: be thine in great revenue! Heaven bless thee from

speaker: THERSITES
text: a tutor, and discipline come not near thee! Let thy

speaker: THERSITES
text: blood be thy direction till thy death; then if she

speaker: THERSITES
text: that lays thee out says thou art a fair corse, I’ll be

speaker: THERSITES
text: sworn and sworn upon ’t she never shrouded any

speaker: THERSITES
text: but lazars. Amen.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Where’s Achilles?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: What, art thou devout? Wast thou in

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: prayer?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Ay. The heavens hear me!

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Amen.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Who’s there?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Thersites, my lord.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Where? Where? O, where?

speaker: ACHILLES
text:
To
                 
                Thersites
                .
               Art thou come? Why, my cheese, my

speaker: ACHILLES
text: digestion, why hast thou not served thyself in to my

speaker: ACHILLES
text: table so many meals? Come, what’s Agamemnon?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Thy commander, Achilles.—Then, tell me,

speaker: THERSITES
text: Patroclus, what’s Achilles?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Thy lord, Thersites. Then, tell me, I pray

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: thee, what’s Thersites?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Thy knower, Patroclus. Then, tell me, Patroclus,

speaker: THERSITES
text: what art thou?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Thou must tell that knowest.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: O tell, tell.

speaker: THERSITES
text: I’ll decline the whole question. Agamemnon

speaker: THERSITES
text: commands Achilles, Achilles is my lord, I am

speaker: THERSITES
text: Patroclus’ knower, and Patroclus is a fool.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: You rascal!

speaker: THERSITES
text: Peace, fool. I have not done.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: He is a privileged man.—Proceed,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Thersites.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Agamemnon is a fool, Achilles is a fool,

speaker: THERSITES
text: Thersites is a fool, and, as aforesaid, Patroclus is a

speaker: THERSITES
text: fool.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Derive this. Come.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Agamemnon is a fool to offer to command

speaker: THERSITES
text: Achilles, Achilles is a fool to be commanded of

speaker: THERSITES
text: Agamemnon, Thersites is a fool to serve such a fool,

speaker: THERSITES
text: and this Patroclus is a fool positive.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Why am I a fool?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Make that demand of the creator. It suffices

speaker: THERSITES
text: me thou art.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Look you, who comes here?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Patroclus, I’ll speak with nobody.—Come in

speaker: ACHILLES
text: with me, Thersites.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Here is such patchery, such juggling, and

speaker: THERSITES
text: such knavery. All the argument is a whore and a

speaker: THERSITES
text: cuckold, a good quarrel to draw emulous factions

speaker: THERSITES
text: and bleed to death upon. Now the dry serpigo on

speaker: THERSITES
text: the subject, and war and lechery confound all!

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Where is Achilles?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Within his tent, but ill-disposed, my lord.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Let it be known to him that we are here.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: He shent our messengers, and we lay by

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Our appertainments, visiting of him.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Let him be told so, lest perchance he think

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We dare not move the question of our place

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Or know not what we are.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: I shall say so to him.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: We saw him at the opening of his tent.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He is not sick.

speaker: AJAX
text: Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart. You may call

speaker: AJAX
text: it melancholy if you will favor the man, but, by my

speaker: AJAX
text: head, ’tis pride. But, why, why? Let him show us a

speaker: AJAX
text: cause.—A word, my lord.

speaker: NESTOR
text: What moves Ajax thus to bay at him?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Achilles hath inveigled his fool from him.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Who, Thersites?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Then will Ajax lack matter, if he have lost his

speaker: NESTOR
text: argument.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: No. You see, he is his argument that has his

speaker: ULYSSES
text: argument: Achilles.

speaker: NESTOR
text: All the better. Their fraction is more our wish

speaker: NESTOR
text: than their faction. But it was a strong composure a

speaker: NESTOR
text: fool could disunite.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may

speaker: ULYSSES
text: easily untie.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Here comes Patroclus.

speaker: NESTOR
text: No Achilles with him.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: The elephant hath joints, but none for courtesy;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: his legs are legs for necessity, not for flexure.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Achilles bids me say he is much sorry

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: If anything more than your sport and pleasure

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Did move your greatness and this noble state

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: To call upon him. He hopes it is no other

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: But for your health and your digestion sake,

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: An after-dinner’s breath.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Hear you, Patroclus:

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We are too well acquainted with these answers,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: But his evasion, winged thus swift with scorn,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Cannot outfly our apprehensions.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Much attribute he hath, and much the reason

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Why we ascribe it to him. Yet all his virtues,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Not virtuously on his own part beheld,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Do in our eyes begin to lose their gloss,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Yea, and like fair fruit in an unwholesome dish,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Are like to rot untasted. Go and tell him

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We come to speak with him; and you shall not sin

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: If you do say we think him overproud

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: And underhonest, in self-assumption greater

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Than in the note of judgment; and worthier than himself

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Here tend the savage strangeness he puts on,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Disguise the holy strength of their command,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: And underwrite in an observing kind

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: His humorous predominance—yea, watch

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: His course and time, his ebbs and flows, as if

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: The passage and whole carriage of this action

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Rode on his tide. Go tell him this, and add

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: That, if he overhold his price so much,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We’ll none of him. But let him, like an engine

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Not portable, lie under this report:

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: “Bring action hither; this cannot go to war.”

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: A stirring dwarf we do allowance give

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Before a sleeping giant. Tell him so.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: I shall, and bring his answer presently.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: In second voice we’ll not be satisfied;

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We come to speak with him.—Ulysses, enter you.

speaker: AJAX
text: What is he more than another?

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: No more than what he thinks he is.

speaker: AJAX
text: Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself

speaker: AJAX
text: a better man than I am?

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: No question.

speaker: AJAX
text: Will you subscribe his thought and say he is?

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: No, noble Ajax. You are as strong, as

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: valiant, as wise, no less noble, much more gentle,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: and altogether more tractable.

speaker: AJAX
text: Why should a man be proud? How doth pride

speaker: AJAX
text: grow? I know not what pride is.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Your mind is the clearer, Ajax, and your

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: virtues the fairer. He that is proud eats up himself.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Pride is his own glass, his own trumpet, his own

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: chronicle; and whatever praises itself but in the

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: deed devours the deed in the praise.

speaker: AJAX
text: I do hate a proud man as I hate the engendering

speaker: AJAX
text: of toads.

speaker: NESTOR
text: And yet he loves himself. Is ’t not strange?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Achilles will not to the field tomorrow.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: What’s his excuse?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He doth rely on none,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: But carries on the stream of his dispose,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Without observance or respect of any,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: In will peculiar and in self-admission.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Why, will he not, upon our fair request,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Untent his person and share th’ air with us?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Things small as nothing, for request’s sake only,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He makes important. Possessed he is with greatness

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And speaks not to himself but with a pride

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That quarrels at self-breath. Imagined worth

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Holds in his blood such swoll’n and hot discourse

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That ’twixt his mental and his active parts

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Kingdomed Achilles in commotion rages

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And batters down himself. What should I say?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He is so plaguy proud that the death-tokens of it

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Cry “No recovery.”

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Let Ajax go to him.—

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: ’Tis said he holds you well and will be led

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: At your request a little from himself.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: O Agamemnon, let it not be so!

speaker: ULYSSES
text: We’ll consecrate the steps that Ajax makes

speaker: ULYSSES
text: When they go from Achilles. Shall the proud lord

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That bastes his arrogance with his own seam

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And never suffers matter of the world

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Enter his thoughts, save such as doth revolve

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And ruminate himself—shall he be worshipped

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Of that we hold an idol more than he?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: No. This thrice-worthy and right valiant lord

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Shall not so stale his palm, nobly acquired,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Nor, by my will, assubjugate his merit,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: As amply titled as Achilles is,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: By going to Achilles.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That were to enlard his fat-already pride

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And add more coals to Cancer when he burns

speaker: ULYSSES
text: With entertaining great Hyperion.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: This lord go to him? Jupiter forbid

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And say in thunder “Achilles, go to him.”

speaker: NESTOR
text: O, this is well; he rubs the vein of him.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: And how his silence drinks up this applause!

speaker: AJAX
text: If I go to him, with my armèd fist

speaker: AJAX
text: I’ll pash him o’er the face.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: O, no, you shall not go.

speaker: AJAX
text: An he be proud with me, I’ll feeze his pride.

speaker: AJAX
text: Let me go to him.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Not for the worth that hangs upon our quarrel.

speaker: AJAX
text: A paltry, insolent fellow.

speaker: NESTOR
text: How he describes himself!

speaker: AJAX
text: Can he not be sociable?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: The raven chides blackness.

speaker: AJAX
text: I’ll let his humorous blood.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: He will be the physician that

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: should be the patient.

speaker: AJAX
text: An all men were of my mind—

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Wit would be out of fashion.

speaker: AJAX
text: —he should not bear it so; he should eat swords

speaker: AJAX
text: first. Shall pride carry it?

speaker: NESTOR
text: An ’twould, you’d carry half.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He would have ten shares.

speaker: AJAX
text: I will knead him; I’ll make him supple.

speaker: NESTOR
text: He’s not yet through warm. Force him

speaker: NESTOR
text: with praises. Pour in, pour in; his ambition is dry.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: My lord, you feed too much on this dislike.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Our noble general, do not do so.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: You must prepare to fight without Achilles.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Why, ’tis this naming of him does him harm.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Here is a man—but ’tis before his face;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: I will be silent.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Wherefore should you so?

speaker: NESTOR
text: He is not emulous, as Achilles is.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Know the whole world, he is as valiant—

speaker: AJAX
text: A whoreson dog, that shall palter with us thus!

speaker: AJAX
text: Would he were a Trojan!

speaker: NESTOR
text: What a vice were it in Ajax now—

speaker: ULYSSES
text: If he were proud—

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Or covetous of praise—

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Ay, or surly borne—

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Or strange, or self-affected—

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Thank the heavens, lord, thou art of sweet composure.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Praise him that gat thee, she that gave thee suck;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Famed be thy tutor, and thy parts of nature

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Thrice famed beyond, beyond thy erudition;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: But he that disciplined thine arms to fight,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Let Mars divide eternity in twain

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And give him half; and for thy vigor,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Bull-bearing Milo his addition yield

speaker: ULYSSES
text: To sinewy Ajax. I will not praise thy wisdom,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Which like a bourn, a pale, a shore confines

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Thy spacious and dilated parts. Here’s Nestor,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Instructed by the antiquary times;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He must, he is, he cannot but be wise.—

speaker: ULYSSES
text: But pardon, father Nestor, were your days

speaker: ULYSSES
text: As green as Ajax’ and your brain so tempered,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: You should not have the eminence of him,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: But be as Ajax.

speaker: AJAX
text: Shall I call you father?

speaker: NESTOR
text: Ay, my good son.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Be ruled by him, Lord Ajax.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: There is no tarrying here; the hart Achilles

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Keeps thicket. Please it our great general

speaker: ULYSSES
text: To call together all his state of war.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Fresh kings are come to Troy. Tomorrow

speaker: ULYSSES
text: We must with all our main of power stand fast.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And here’s a lord—come knights from east to west

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And cull their flower, Ajax shall cope the best.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Go we to council. Let Achilles sleep.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Light boats sail swift, though greater hulks draw deep.
